title: The Problem of Evil: Philosophy's Hardest Question
body:
The problem of evil is the oldest and most persistent challenge to theistic belief: if God is omnipotent (all-powerful), omniscient (all-knowing), and omnibenevolent (all-good), why does suffering exist? Why do children develop terminal illnesses? Why do earthquakes kill thousands? Why does the capacity for cruelty appear to be part of the human endowment?

The logical form of the problem, as sharpened by philosophers from David Hume to J.L. Mackie, claims that these three divine attributes are logically incompatible with the existence of suffering: an omnipotent God could prevent it, an omniscient God would know how, and an omnibenevolent God would want to. Since suffering exists, at least one of the three attributes must be false — which means the God of classical theism does not exist.

The major responses — theodicies, as they are called — attempt to explain why an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God might have reasons to permit suffering that we cannot fully perceive. The free will defence argues that genuine freedom requires the real possibility of wrong choice, and that a world containing free creatures who can choose evil is better than a world of determined creatures who cannot. The soul-making theodicy (associated with John Hick) argues that suffering is the necessary context for the development of virtues — courage, compassion, endurance — that give life its deepest meaning.

Neither response fully satisfies its critics. The free will defence addresses moral evil (evil produced by human choices) but not natural evil (earthquakes, disease). The soul-making theodicy seems to require an implausibly large quantity of suffering to produce its educational benefits. The problem remains genuinely hard — which is part of what makes it philosophically important.

title: Buddhist Philosophy and the Self: What It Actually Teaches About Consciousness
body:
The Buddhist doctrine of anatta — no-self — is among the most radical and most misunderstood propositions in the history of philosophy. It does not claim that you do not exist. It claims that what you take yourself to be — a unified, persistent self that is the subject of experience and the owner of consciousness — is a conceptual construction rather than a metaphysical reality.

The Buddhist analysis of what appears to be the self identifies five aggregates: form (the physical body and its sense organs), sensation (the felt quality of experience), perception (the recognition of objects), mental formations (volitions, emotions, habits), and consciousness (awareness). None of these, individually or collectively, constitutes a permanent, unified self. What we call 'I' is a process — a constantly changing flow of interdependent physical and mental events — that the mind habituates to labelling as a thing.

The practical implications the Buddha drew from this analysis are specific: if the self is a construction rather than a fixed reality, then the suffering produced by defending and aggrandising that self is unnecessary. Liberation — nirvana — is not the annihilation of the self but the end of the compulsive clinging to a self that never existed as we imagined it.

Contemporary philosophy of mind has approached similar territory through entirely different routes. Derek Parfit's work on personal identity — particularly his conclusion that personal identity is not what matters, and that what matters is psychological continuity — reaches conclusions strikingly similar to Buddhist anatta. The convergence between ancient contemplative analysis and contemporary analytic philosophy suggests that both are tracking something real about the nature of consciousness and identity.

title: Existentialism: What Sartre, Camus, and Beauvoir Actually Believed
body:
Existentialism — the philosophical movement centred on the individual's freedom, responsibility, and confrontation with existence — has generated more distortion than perhaps any other philosophical school. The distortions typically run in two directions: a romantic distortion (existentialism as the philosophy of dark coffee and brooding in Paris) and a nihilistic distortion (existentialism as the view that nothing matters). Neither is accurate.

Sartre's central claim — 'existence precedes essence' — means that human beings, unlike manufactured objects, do not arrive with a predetermined purpose or nature. A table is made for something; its essence (what it is for) precedes its existence. A human being simply exists first, and then creates their essence through their choices and commitments. This is not cause for despair but for responsibility: we are radically free, and that freedom is inescapable.

Albert Camus, often associated with existentialism though he rejected the label, developed a complementary position around the concept of the absurd: the conflict between the human desire for meaning and the universe's silence on the question. His response to the absurd was not nihilism but revolt — the decision to live fully and passionately in the face of meaninglessness, to embrace the absurd as the condition of an authentic life.

Simone de Beauvoir applied existentialist principles to ethics and feminism, arguing that the freedom that existentialism demands must be pursued not only for oneself but for all others — that genuine freedom is incompatible with the oppression of others, and that the liberation of women was a prerequisite for any authentic human freedom. Her contribution to existentialism is both philosophically substantial and historically undervalued.

title: Mysticism Across Traditions: What the Contemplatives Agree On
body:
The perennial philosophy — the idea that beneath the enormous diversity of the world's religious traditions there is a common core of mystical experience — was articulated in modern form by Aldous Huxley but has a much older history. The observation that Christian mystics, Sufi masters, Buddhist contemplatives, Jewish Kabbalists, and Hindu sages describe strikingly similar experiences of unity, selflessness, and direct encounter with the ground of reality has impressed thinkers from Leibniz to William James.

The common features are specific. The experience of unity — the dissolution of the ordinary sense of separation between self and world, self and other, self and the ultimate reality — is the most consistently reported feature. The ineffability of the experience: it cannot be adequately described in ordinary language. The noetic quality: it carries a sense of having directly perceived something true about the nature of reality. The transience: the experience does not persist, though its effects may last a lifetime. The sense of sacredness or presence.

Sceptics have argued that the apparent similarity of mystical experiences across traditions is an artifact of interpretation — that what is actually universal is the category of 'mystical experience', which then gets interpreted through the conceptual frameworks of each tradition. The Christian mystic experiences God; the Buddhist meditator experiences sunyata (emptiness); the Hindu yogi experiences Brahman. These may be the same experience described in different vocabularies, or they may be genuinely different experiences that share surface similarities.

The question matters beyond academic interest: if the contemplative traditions do converge on a common reality, it suggests that human consciousness has genuine access to dimensions of existence that ordinary awareness does not reveal — a possibility with significant implications for how we understand the nature of mind.

title: Free Will and Determinism: Does Your Choice Actually Matter?
body:
The free will debate is not an abstract philosophical puzzle. Its answer determines whether moral responsibility, guilt, punishment, and praise are rationally justified. If our choices are entirely determined by prior causes — our genes, our upbringing, our neural states at the moment of decision — then in what sense are we responsible for them? And if we are not responsible, what justifies treating people differently based on the choices they make?

Hard determinism argues that all events, including human choices, are the inevitable products of prior causes operating according to physical laws. The feeling of freedom — of genuinely having been able to choose otherwise — is an illusion produced by our inability to introspect our own causal processes. This position is internally consistent but has consequences that many find unacceptable: it appears to undermine the rational basis for moral responsibility.

Libertarian free will (not to be confused with the political position) argues that human choices are genuinely undetermined — that some property of human consciousness (consciousness itself, perhaps, or quantum indeterminacy) allows for genuine origination of choice not fully explained by prior causes. This view preserves moral responsibility but faces the challenge of explaining how a random or undetermined choice is more free than a determined one.

Compatibilism — the dominant position among contemporary philosophers — argues that free will and determinism are compatible, because 'free will' properly understood does not require the ability to have done otherwise, but rather the ability to act in accordance with one's own desires and reasons without external coercion. On this view, you act freely when you do what you want to do, even if your wanting is itself determined. Critics argue that this is a redefinition of free will rather than a solution to the problem.

title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence: What Philosophy Has to Say
body:
The development of artificial intelligence presents philosophy with some of its most urgent practical questions. What moral status, if any, should be accorded to AI systems? Who bears responsibility when an AI system causes harm? How should the benefits and risks of AI be distributed? Can a machine have genuine interests that deserve moral consideration?

The question of moral status depends on the criteria we use to assign it. Sentience-based views — associated with Peter Singer and utilitarian ethics — hold that the capacity to suffer is the relevant criterion. If an AI system can genuinely suffer (a question on which there is no current scientific consensus), it would warrant moral consideration on this view. Rational agency-based views — associated with Kantian ethics — hold that the capacity for rational autonomous choice is the criterion. Whether current AI systems meet this standard is similarly contested.

The responsibility question is practically urgent: when a self-driving vehicle causes a fatal accident, or when a medical diagnostic AI misses a cancer, who is accountable? Existing legal and moral frameworks for responsibility were developed in a world of human agents making human decisions. They do not map neatly onto systems in which decisions emerge from the interaction of training data, model architecture, deployment context, and the choices of users who may be distant from the development process.

The distributive question is perhaps the most immediately consequential: the productivity gains from AI will accrue primarily to those who own the systems, while the displacement costs will be borne primarily by those whose labour the systems replace. Classical philosophical frameworks — from Rawlsian justice to utilitarian welfare economics — provide different and sometimes incompatible guidance on how these gains and costs should be shared.

title: Stoicism in the Modern World: What Actually Applies
body:
Stoicism has enjoyed a remarkable popular revival in the past two decades, with Marcus Aurelius's Meditations regularly appearing on lists of books recommended by Silicon Valley founders, athletes, and military leaders. The appeal is understandable: a philosophy developed for navigating the enormous uncertainties and responsibilities of Roman imperial power translates quite readily to the pressures of high-stakes contemporary life. But the popular reception often selects only the most immediately applicable elements while setting aside the broader theoretical framework that gives these practices their coherence.

The Stoic tripartite division of human concern — into things fully in our power, things partially in our power, and things not in our power — is the cornerstone of the practice. Epictetus identified this distinction as the beginning of wisdom: the source of most human suffering is the attachment of our wellbeing to outcomes we do not fully control. Health, reputation, wealth, others' opinions — all are partially within our influence but not fully within our control. The Stoic practice involves progressively relocating the ground of our wellbeing from these externals to the one domain that is genuinely our own: the quality of our judgments, intentions, and responses.

The modern reception has sometimes been criticised, fairly, for reducing Stoicism to a personal productivity philosophy that ignores its political and communal dimensions. The Stoics were cosmopolitans before the concept had a name: Marcus Aurelius's Meditations is full of reminders that we are members of a community extending beyond family, city, and empire — a community of rational beings. The cultivation of equanimity was never intended to produce passive acceptance of injustice but to enable clear-headed engagement with it. This dimension of Stoic thought is at least as relevant to contemporary life as the personal resilience for which it is more commonly cited.

title: Buddhism and the Nature of Mind
body:
The central claim of Buddhist philosophy — that the self we normally take ourselves to be is not what we think it is — is among the most radical propositions in the history of human thought, and one that has found increasing resonance with certain strands of contemporary neuroscience and philosophy of mind. The Buddhist doctrine of anatta (non-self) does not claim that nothing exists — it claims that what exists is a constantly changing process rather than a permanent, independent entity. The sense of being a stable, continuous self is, on this account, a construction — a story the mind tells itself — rather than a direct perception of what is actually there.

The contemplative practices developed to investigate and ultimately realise this insight — meditation in its various forms, but also the practices of inquiry associated with Zen and Tibetan traditions — are not merely calming techniques. They are, at their most rigorous, epistemological investigations: attempts to look directly at the nature of experience and examine whether the usual assumptions about self, world, and their relationship hold up under careful examination. The consistency of what is reported by practitioners who have engaged these practices seriously, across widely separated traditions and historical periods, is one of the more striking facts about human consciousness.

The dialogue between Buddhism and Western philosophy and science has been one of the intellectually richest developments of the past half-century. The Mind and Life Institute, founded by Francisco Varela and the Dalai Lama, has facilitated sustained collaborative inquiry that has produced genuine insights in both directions. Buddhist concepts — mindfulness, the investigation of consciousness, the relationship between compassion and well-being — have entered mainstream scientific discourse, while scientific findings about brain plasticity have begun to influence how Buddhist teachers present their tradition to contemporary audiences.

title: The Problem of Evil: A Philosophical Tour
body:
The problem of evil — the question of how an omnipotent, omniscient, and omnibenevolent God can coexist with the suffering and moral evil that clearly exist in the world — has occupied theologians and philosophers for millennia and shows no sign of resolution. This is not because the problem is trivial or because those who have engaged it were unintelligent. It is because the problem cuts to the heart of what it means to hold any theodicy, and because the available responses all have genuine costs alongside their benefits.

The free will defence — the argument that God permits moral evil as the necessary cost of human freedom — is the most developed and influential response. Alvin Plantinga's sophisticated formulation established that the logical version of the problem of evil (the claim that an omnipotent God and evil are logically incompatible) is unsuccessful. But critics point out that the free will defence does not address natural evil — the suffering caused by disease, natural disaster, and predation — which is unconnected to human choice.

The evidential problem of evil, championed by philosophers like William Rowe and Paul Draper, concedes that evil and God are not logically incompatible but argues that the amount and distribution of suffering constitutes significant evidence against the existence of a benevolent God. The theistic responses — sceptical theism, the soul-making theodicy, eschatological justification — each have genuine philosophical weight and genuine weaknesses. The problem of evil is one of the most serious challenges to classical theism, and engaging with it seriously is essential for any honest religious philosophy.

title: Existentialism: A Practical Philosophy
body:
Existentialism, despite its reputation for producing paralysing anxiety, began as a philosophy of liberation. Søren Kierkegaard was writing against the comfortable accommodation between official Christianity and bourgeois Danish society that produced a population who had outsourced the most important questions of their lives to institutions and conventions rather than facing them directly. The demand for authentic existence — for taking genuine responsibility for one's choices rather than hiding behind the excuse of social expectation — is not an invitation to despair. It is a form of respect for human dignity.

Sartre's famous declaration that 'existence precedes essence' — that human beings have no predetermined nature that defines what they should be, but must create their own essence through their choices — is both exhilarating and vertiginous. It removes the safety net of a given meaning but also removes the constraints of a given nature. We are not born with a fixed purpose that we must discover and fulfil; we are free, radically free, to choose what we will make of ourselves. The nausea this produces in Sartre's fictional characters is the flip side of a freedom that, once genuinely apprehended, cannot be unfelt.

The existentialist tradition's most practically useful contribution may be its insistence on taking seriously the experience of anxiety as a philosophical signal rather than a psychological symptom to be managed. Kierkegaard called anxiety 'the dizziness of freedom' — the sensation that arises when we genuinely confront the openness of our situation. Heidegger's analysis of anxiety as the mood in which our usual evasions of genuine existence fail is not a clinical description but a phenomenological one: anxiety, properly attended to, can be the beginning of a more authentic orientation toward life rather than merely an uncomfortable experience to be relieved.

title: Indigenous Wisdom Traditions: What They Offer the Modern World
body:
The indigenous wisdom traditions of the world have survived, in many cases, thousands of years of colonial pressure, missionary suppression, forced assimilation, and deliberate cultural destruction. That survival is itself a kind of testament to their vitality and value. These traditions represent the accumulated observational and practical wisdom of peoples living in intimate relationship with specific landscapes and ecosystems over centuries or millennia. They contain ecological knowledge, medical knowledge, philosophical knowledge, and social wisdom that the dominant globalised culture has not replicated and in some cases cannot replicate.

The relationship between indigenous wisdom traditions and Western academic knowledge has been historically extractive: Western researchers have taken traditional knowledge without acknowledgment, compensation, or reciprocal sharing. The most productive encounters between traditional and scientific knowledge systems have occurred when both are genuinely respected: complementary, each offering what the other lacks, each capable of being enriched by genuine dialogue.

What indigenous traditions most consistently offer the contemporary world is a set of perspectives on the relationship between human beings and the rest of the natural world that the dominant culture has largely lost: the sense that human beings are participants in rather than managers of ecological systems, that wisdom requires relationship with specific places rather than abstract principles applicable anywhere, and that the boundaries between human and non-human are more porous than Western modernity has assumed. These perspectives are not romantic nostalgia — they are correctives to demonstrably damaging assumptions, and they are urgently needed.

title: The Tao Te Ching: Ancient Wisdom for Modern Life
body:
The Tao Te Ching — attributed to the sage Laozi and composed, most scholars believe, in the fourth or third century BCE — is one of the shortest and most widely translated texts in world literature. Its eighty-one brief chapters, totalling fewer than 5,000 Chinese characters, have generated commentary and interpretation across twenty-five centuries and in virtually every major language. The text's brevity is not a limitation but a feature: the Tao — the Way — cannot be fully articulated in language, and Laozi's compressed, paradoxical style is a deliberate pointing toward what language cannot contain.

The core teaching is the principle of wu wei — often translated as 'non-action' or 'effortless action' — which is less about inactivity than about action that flows with the nature of things rather than against them. Water, in Laozi's recurrent image, does not strive or force, yet over time it shapes the hardest stone. The leader who leads with wu wei does not dominate but facilitates; the craftsman who works with wu wei does not impose their will on the material but allows the material's own nature to guide the work. This is not passivity. It is the highest form of responsiveness.

The contemporary relevance of the Tao Te Ching lies precisely in its resistance to the dominant assumptions of Western modernity: that value is produced through effort and control, that complexity is superior to simplicity, that mastery means domination rather than understanding. These assumptions are producing consequences — environmental, psychological, social — that the text's insights directly address. Reading Laozi carefully is an exercise in questioning premises so fundamental that they have become invisible, which is perhaps why the text has found readers in every generation since it was composed.

title: What Does it Mean to Live a Good Life? Philosophy's Answers
body:
The question of how to live well is the oldest question in philosophy and the one to which the greatest variety of compelling answers has been offered. Aristotle argued that the good life is one in which we fulfil our nature as rational, social beings — a life of virtuous activity in community, the development and exercise of our distinctively human capacities. The Epicureans argued that the good life is one characterised by pleasures of the mind and freedom from anxiety — ataraxia, tranquillity — rather than the pursuit of intense pleasures that carry intense consequences. The Stoics located the good exclusively in virtue, maintaining that all external circumstances, however terrible, leave the inner life unaffected if properly oriented.

Each of these ancient answers identifies something genuine. Aristotle's insistence on social embeddedness and the importance of genuinely exercising human capacities — not merely having them — is supported by contemporary psychological research on meaning and well-being. The Epicurean insight that many of the things we most ardently pursue produce less satisfaction than simpler alternatives is confirmed by the hedonic adaptation research of positive psychology. The Stoic insight that the quality of our relationship to circumstances matters more than the circumstances themselves is one of the most empirically well-supported claims in the psychology of well-being.

The fact that these ancient answers partially converge — all of them emphasising some version of virtue, of appropriate relationship to material circumstances, and of engagement with others — is itself instructive. It suggests that philosophical reflection, across very different cultural contexts and historical periods, tends to arrive at a recognisable territory: a life oriented toward what genuinely matters, conducted with integrity, embedded in community, and characterised by the development and exercise of distinctively human capacities. This territory is worth inhabiting deliberately.

title: Meditation Across Traditions: What They Share
body:
Meditation — the deliberate cultivation of particular qualities of attention and awareness through regular practice — appears in virtually every major religious and philosophical tradition in human history. Buddhist vipassana and samatha, Christian contemplative prayer, Jewish hitbonenut, Islamic dhikr, Hindu dhyana, Sufi sama — these are not the same practice, and the theological frameworks surrounding them differ fundamentally. But they share a family resemblance sufficiently strong to suggest that they are all working with something real in the structure of human consciousness, approaching it from different angles with different instruments.

What these traditions most consistently share is the insight that ordinary waking consciousness is not the whole of what is available to human awareness. There is a dimension of awareness that is prior to thought, prior to the sense of being a separate self, prior to the emotional reactivity that dominates most waking experience. The practices that cultivate access to this dimension do so through the deliberate training of attention — typically through some form of concentrated, sustained, non-reactive awareness of present-moment experience. The territory accessed through this training is described in different theological languages, but the descriptions share enough features to suggest they are pointing at the same thing.

For the contemporary practitioner who is not embedded in any particular religious tradition, this convergence is both liberating and challenging. Liberating because it suggests that the fruits of contemplative practice are not the exclusive property of any tradition. Challenging because the traditions typically insist — with what appears to be practical wisdom — that sustained progress requires a stable framework of practice, guidance from experienced teachers, and a community of fellow practitioners. Shopping between traditions without committing deeply to any of them may produce pleasant experiences while failing to produce genuine transformation.

title: Ethics in the Age of AI: New Questions for Old Philosophy
body:
The rapid development of artificial intelligence is generating ethical questions that existing moral frameworks address only partially, and sometimes not at all. Some of these questions are extensions of familiar problems — how to distribute the benefits and burdens of a new technology, how to hold powerful actors accountable, how to protect the vulnerable from exploitation. These are important questions, and existing tools in political philosophy, legal theory, and applied ethics are genuinely useful in addressing them. But artificial intelligence also raises genuinely novel questions about the nature of moral status, the attribution of responsibility, and the meaning of human identity that require new thinking.

The question of moral status — whether AI systems have morally relevant interests that create obligations on the part of those who create and deploy them — is not one that can be answered by analogy to existing categories. The criteria we use to attribute moral status to humans and animals — consciousness, the capacity for suffering, the possession of preferences that can be frustrated — are exactly the criteria whose applicability to AI systems is most uncertain. As AI systems become more sophisticated in their behaviour and more opaque in their internal workings, the uncertainty about their inner life will not decrease. This uncertainty itself has moral implications that philosophers are only beginning to work through.

The question of responsibility for AI-generated harm is equally novel. When an AI system causes harm — by providing dangerous advice, by making a discriminatory decision, by being used as a tool for fraud or manipulation — who bears moral and legal responsibility? The developer who created the system? The deployer who applied it in a particular context? The user who directed it? The system itself? These are not merely legal questions — they reflect deep puzzles about agency, causation, and the attribution of blame that existing moral philosophy was not designed to answer. Engaging with them seriously is one of the most important intellectual tasks of the present moment.

title: Finding Meaning Without Religion: Secular Approaches
body:
The decline of institutional religious participation in most Western societies has not been accompanied by a decline in the hunger for meaning, purpose, and transcendence that religious traditions have historically provided. What has emerged instead is a complex landscape of secular meaning-making: therapeutic culture, political commitment, artistic practice, athletic achievement, parenthood, and various forms of spirituality that draw on religious resources without institutional commitment. The question of whether these secular alternatives adequately replace what religion provided — and whether 'replacement' is even the right frame — is one of the most significant questions in contemporary social life.

Alain de Botton's 'Religion for Atheists' argues, controversially, that secular culture has been too hasty in discarding the institutional and practical wisdom accumulated by religious traditions — their techniques for building community, marking life transitions, cultivating virtues, and providing comfort in the face of mortality. The argument is not that the metaphysical claims of religion should be accepted, but that the practices and institutions those claims motivated served genuine human needs that secular culture has not yet adequately replaced.

The philosophical tradition of secular humanism offers a framework for meaning that is explicit about its rejection of supernaturalism while preserving the core commitments — to human dignity, to the importance of reason and evidence, to the cultivation of virtue and the pursuit of human flourishing — that underlie the best in religious ethics. For many people, this framework is sufficient. For others, something is missing that philosophical argument cannot supply. The conversation between those who find secular meaning adequate and those who do not is one of the most important conversations of our time, and it requires honesty from both sides about what is genuinely at stake.
